text.title: Regulatory Uncertainty Buffer — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `regulatory-uncertainty-buffer` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a buffer from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: The decision rule names its inputs (signal, threshold, source). 'Use judgment' is not an input.

rationale.source: Structured decision-making (Kahneman, Noise); checklist-driven decision practice

rule.rationale: Unnamed inputs let bias dominate. Named inputs make the call reproducible and challengeable.

rule.statement: Each branch carries a published threshold (numeric or boolean). 'Significant' and 'material' are rejected without a number.

rationale.source: Outcome-based decision practice; safety-critical thresholding (NTSB, FAA)

rule.rationale: Threshold-free rules become whatever the decider felt that morning. Published thresholds make the rule a tool, not folklore.

rule.statement: The rule has a documented default for the unhappy path (timeout, missing data, ambiguous signal). Silent defaults are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Most decisions fail not on the happy path but on edge cases. A documented default removes ambiguity and shortens the meeting.

rule.statement: Every invocation is logged with inputs, branch taken, and decider. The log is reviewable by anyone affected.
rule.rationale: Audit trails for decision rules turn a rule into an organisational asset. Without the log the rule is one person's opinion stamped with a name.

rule.statement: If a logged call produces a bad outcome, the rule itself is reviewed in the next retro. The rule is not assumed correct.
rule.rationale: Static rules rot. The review-on-failure loop is the cheap mechanism that keeps the rule calibrated to reality.
